lede: A read-only market record of Kim Asendorf's onchain collections across Ethereum and Tezos — contracts, supply, floors, holders, volumes and lending-market status, assembled from public onchain and marketplace data.
metric_note: Marketplace “all-time volume” figures are OpenSea secondary volume only. Primary mints on custom contracts are invisible to that metric — where reconstructed on-chain, primary proceeds are shown separately.

token.name: Pixel
token.lede: One fungible token threads Asendorf's post-2024 work: PXL. The PXL DEX and PXL POD collections above are its containers — every deck and pod carries attached pixels, and the deck contract is the token's only primary market, minting at a fixed 0.000001 ETH per PXL. No AMM pool exists; to move size in PXL, you move the container.
notes: All token figures read directly from the contracts on 2026-07-06: cap(), totalSupply(), balanceOf on the two art contracts, per-deck mint allowance summed over decks 1–256, and pixelPrice().
Free float = minted supply minus PXL held by the two art contracts, i.e. PXL sitting loose in wallets.

PXL NET opens July 11, 2026 at NODE, Palo Alto (nodefoundation.com). Artist-stated mechanics (Discord, 2026-07-04–08; contract not yet deployed — to be verified on-chain): the net holds up to 128,000,000 PXL; visitors buy PXL at the exhibition from $8 (4,096 PXL) to $2,560 (1,280,000 PXL) — a flat ~$0.002 per PXL, roughly the deck-mint parity in USD; sale proceeds remain at NODE as a fundraiser, with 5% donated to panke.gallery, Berlin. Participation is in-person only (RFID cards + a NODE app smart wallet; each buyer has their own wallet, NODE holds a spend approval). Adding PXL to the net issues a generative receipt NFT ('PXL NODE', 100–31,250 total, 24 properties across shapes/animations/tweens, randomized on-chain). Receipts are immutable — no deposits or withdrawals — but can be burned through the contract to redeem the underlying PXL: the burn destroys the artwork and returns the tokens, a redemption right, not a supply sink. Token 0 displays all PXL in the net without owning them.

editorial.title: The desk's read
editorial.sub: A chronology of the practice since 2022, and the questions it leaves open
editorial.throughline: The through-line this desk keeps returning to: Asendorf publishes his market structure the way he publishes his images. The technique that made his name — pixel sorting — has been open source since 2012; the economy around the recent work is authored as deliberately as the works themselves, down to a hard cap that writes the ending into the contract. Somewhere in this chronology the economy stops being a distribution channel and becomes part of the medium.

timeline.text: Proof of People, Bright Moments London. This desk's first direct contact with the practice.
Event Horizon: 99 works, shown in Tokyo. The smallest of the Ethereum collections and still among the tightest-held.
ASDF. The economics still sit outside the work — a conventional release, no token attached. The last project before that changes.
X0X: a 1,000-supply drop — the largest edition since Cargo, and the last collection released before the PXL system.
PXL DEX: 256 decks sell out. Each deck carries attached PXL, an ERC-20 hard-capped at 1,024,000,000, and the deck contract is the token's only primary market. The economy is now inside the work.
PXL POD: 256 pods at 1 ETH plus attached PXL. Asendorf, to Le Random: "You should feel a risk to participate."
Every Asendorf Ethereum collection is now listed loan collateral on Gondi. Within the set, the PXL works carry the highest floors and the lowest turnover.

PXL NET announced for July 11, 2026 at NODE, Palo Alto (nodefoundation.com). Per the artist (Discord, early July): up to 128M PXL, sold in person from $8/4,096 to $2,560/1,280,000 — about $0.002 per PXL, parity extended to a counter price — proceeds to NODE with 5% to panke.gallery. Participation issues a generative receipt NFT that can be burned to redeem the underlying PXL. Token 0 is the network itself. The contract is not yet deployed; this record verifies those mechanics on-chain once it is.

questions: When an artist designs the market for the work as carefully as the work itself, who is the author of that economy — and is collecting in it participation or performance?
Is PXL the artwork and the decks and pods its display layer — or the reverse? The contracts don't answer this; the price structure might.
The technique is free — anyone can pixel-sort. The economy is scarce by design. What does it mean that this inversion of the usual arrangement is the point?
If NET's receipts can only be burned whole, the burn record becomes a public ledger of conviction — judgment rendered empirically, onchain. Who will it judge?
The hard cap means the system knows how it ends. What is a collector's position in a work whose conclusion is already written?
editorial.note: Editorial context, not market data. Chronology dates are from the public record; the reading is the desk's own. PXL NET mechanics reflect the artist's public statements and will be re-verified against the deployed contract before this record states them as fact.

feedback.title: Wishlist
feedback.sub: tell the desk what would make this more useful
feedback.prompt: This page exists for the collectors around the work. If there's a number you wish it tracked, a view you wish it had, or something it gets wrong, say so — notes shape what gets built next, starting with the PXL NET extension.
feedback.placeholder: What should this page track or show that it doesn't yet?

collections.name: PXL POD
flags: Floor indicative — only 2 lifetime secondary sales, none since 2026-03-12.

secondary_sales.note: accepted offer, during the mint window

primary.note: Verified on-chain via Etherscan — the contract-paid portion only. Every one of the 126 paid mints priced identically: exactly 1 ETH plus the attached PXL at the fixed deck rate (0.000001 ETH/PXL), with zero deviation. The artist confirms this structure — “240 x 1 eth plus Pxl” — and reports total primary was roughly 380 ETH including OTC and gallery sales (Kim Asendorf, 2026-07-06). The 240 pods at 1 ETH beyond the 126 settled on-contract were delivered off-contract: the buyer pays the artist's wallet and the pod is owner-minted straight to them. DENZA's wallet-flow reconstruction corroborates the total — direct payments into artist-controlled wallets during the mint window, a substantial share matched one-to-one to owner-minted or artist-transferred pod deliveries, plus stablecoin settlements. Among the off-contract sales were 15 Duo Pods placed through Nguyen Wahed gallery at $15,000 each (artist-reported).
Complete primary — all 256 tokens accounted for on-chain (243 paid + 13 artist/allowlist mints); verified via Etherscan reconciled against ERC-721 mint events.

collections.name: Monogrid
flags: Hand-minted 1/1s on the shared hic et nunc contract; figures are objkt gallery aggregates.
Floor is the lowest active 1/1 ask; treat as indicative.

method: Contracts resolved from the artist's OpenSea creator listing (kimasendorf) and cross-checked against the artist's own catalog (eth.kimasendorf.com); Tezos via the objkt creator record.
USD figures are converted at snapshot spot rates (ETH $1,759.26, XTZ $0.24 — CoinGecko, 2026-07-03) and are indicative only; the ETH/XTZ figures are primary.
Floors, holder counts and secondary volumes from OpenSea stats (Ethereum) and objkt gallery aggregates (Tezos), snapshot 2026-07-03.

PXL POD primary proceeds reconstructed from Etherscan: every successful value-bearing transaction into the mint contract; internal-transaction inflows verified zero. Price distribution cross-checks exactly against pxl.onl's published mint stats.
Where the artist has corrected or extended a figure directly, that statement is quoted with attribution and date — artist-reported numbers are never merged into verified on-chain figures.
Lending status from Gondi's public GraphQL API (active collection offers at snapshot).
caveats: Secondary volumes are single-venue (OpenSea for Ethereum, objkt for Tezos); cross-marketplace reconciliation is pending.

PXL POD's 194.45 ETH primary is the on-chain contract-paid portion only. Per the artist (2026-07-06), the pods sold at a flat 1 ETH plus their attached PXL — 240 pods at that price — for a total primary of roughly 380 ETH including OTC and gallery sales, among them 15 Duo Pods placed through Nguyen Wahed gallery (Art Basel Hong Kong, March 2026) at $15,000 each. The 1-ETH-plus-PXL structure is verified exactly on the 126 on-contract mints; the pod counts, Duo Pod pricing and gallery figures are artist-reported. Wallet-flow reconstruction (2026-07-06) supports the total: roughly 85 ETH of off-contract payments match one-to-one to pod deliveries; a further ~106 ETH plus ~57.5k USDC of mint-window inflows fit the OTC pattern (payment wallet differs from receiving wallet, or settlement routed through an exchange) and are reported as consistent-with rather than proven per-pod.
PXL DEX's 199.49 ETH primary is complete (all 256 tokens on-chain) but rests on the on-chain reconstruction alone — pxl.onl publishes the mint count, not proceeds.
Primary-proceeds reconstruction for the remaining collections (Sabotage, Cargo, Event Horizon, Alternate, X0X), monthly volume series, holder concentration and wash-trade screening are Phase 2 items.
Thin-floor collections (PXL POD, Monogrid) should be treated as indicative, not established.

notice.kicker: pxl net — opens friday, july 11 at NODE, palo alto
notice.text: Pixels sold in person from $8 (4,096 PXL) to $2,560 (1,280,000) — proceeds fund NODE, 5% to panke.gallery. Receipts are generative NFTs that burn to redeem the underlying PXL. Artist-stated; this record verifies the contract on-chain once it's live.

frontispiece.caption: Pixel Pod 69 — PXL POD, 2026. 1,000,000 attached PXL. From the desk's own book.

voices.title: Collector voices
voices.sub: why people hold this work — in their own words, credited
voices.invite: Answer in the ASDF Discord or through the wishlist box below: what's your favorite work of Kim's that you own, and why that one? Selected answers appear here, credited (or anonymous if you prefer).
